29: akoue Israel, kyrios ho theos hemon kyrios heis estin — Hear, O Israel: the Lord our God, the Lord is one — Mark's version of the greatest commandment uniquely includes the Shema itself (Deut 6:4) as the foundation for the love-command. The scribe affirms Jesus's answer and adds that this love is more than all burnt offerings and sacrifices (v.33, citing Hos 6:6 / 1 Sam 15:22), and Jesus responds that the scribe is not far from the kingdom of God. This is Mark's only positive scribal encounter — the exception that proves the rule of official opposition.
1: ampelourgos / phragmon / hypolēnion / pyrgon — vineyard / wall / pit / tower — the parable's opening vocabulary closely mirrors LXX Isaiah 5:1–2 (ōdē tou agapētou, the song of the beloved): Isaiah's vineyard allegory names Israel as the LORD's vineyard. Jesus activates that intertextual memory immediately. The Greek parabolas (parables, plural) may anticipate the series of controversies, though only this parable is narrated. Geōrgois (tenant-farmers, sharecroppers) sets up the social dynamic of absentee landowner and working tenants.
2: apostellō / doulos — sent / slave-servant — apostellō is the same verb from which apostolos (apostle) derives. Here it describes the repeated sending of prophetic servants. Doulos (slave, servant) was the standard LXX term for the prophets as servants of God (Amos 3:7: paideias autou tous doulous autou tous prophētas). The allegorical layer is immediately visible.
3: dero — beat/flog — dero literally means to flay or skin (related to derma, skin). It is a graphic verb for violent treatment, used for the prophet beaten and sent back empty-handed (kenon). The emptiness (kenon) reverses the expectation of fruit (karpon) in v.2.
4: ekephaliōsan kai ētimasan — they struck him on the head and dishonored him — ekephaliōsan (they struck on the head) is a rare verb, possibly coined from kephalē (head). The Greek textual tradition shows variants here; some manuscripts read ekthalamōsan (handled shamefully/wounded). The cumulative pattern of intensifying violence against successive servants mirrors the Deuteronomistic history's account of Israel's rejection of prophets.
5: tous men... tous de — some... others — the correlative construction (tous men derontes, tous de apokteinontes) creates a chiastic escalation: beating escalates to killing. The parable does not individualize each servant; the pattern is corporate — Israel's repeated rejection of the prophetic word.
6: agapēton huion / eschatōs — a beloved son / last of all — agapēton (beloved, dear one) directly echoes the voice at Jesus's baptism (1:11: ho huios mou ho agapētos) and transfiguration (9:7). The allegorical identification of the son with Jesus is transparent. Eschatōs (last of all) signals the finality of this sending — there is no further recourse after the son. Hoti entrapēsontai ton huion mou (they will respect my son) — the owner's reasoning proves tragically mistaken.
7: deute apokteinōmen auton / klēronomia — come let us kill him / the inheritance — the tenants' deliberation (pros heautous, among themselves) exposes the logic of self-serving violence: if the heir is removed, the inheritance passes to them. The Greek word klēronomia (inheritance, patrimony) carries covenantal weight — it is the term for Israel's land inheritance in the LXX.
8: exebalon exō tou ampelōnos — they threw him outside the vineyard — note the order in Mark: killed first, then threw out. Luke 20:15 reverses this (threw out, then killed), perhaps to echo Isa 5:5's vineyard-demolition imagery more explicitly. Mark's order — killed inside, then thrown out — may reflect the reality that Jesus was crucified outside the city walls (Heb 13:12), a detail the Markan audience would recognize.
9: apolesai / dōsei ton ampelōna allois — to destroy / will give the vineyard to others — apolesai (to destroy, to kill) echoes the tenants' own action against the son. The owner's judgment is retributive in kind. Allois (to others) is theologically loaded: the vineyard/covenant-community passes to a new people. This is the parable's climax.
10: lithos hon apedokimasan hoi oikodomountes, houtos egenēthē eis kephalēn gōnias — the stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone — Psalm 118:22 citation. This is the same Psalm whose hōsanna and eulogēmenos ho erchomenos (118:25–26) were shouted in the triumphal entry (11:9–10), creating a tight intertextual frame around the temple controversies. Apedokimasan (they rejected — from dokimazō, to test and reject as unworthy) is the builders' verdict, overturned by God. Kephalē gōnias (head of the corner) denotes the foundational stone that locks two walls together.
11: para kyriou egeneto hautē / thaumastē — this is from the Lord / wonderful/marvelous — Psalm 118:23. The divine reversal is not merely surprising but thaumastē (marvelous, awe-inspiring). The LXX quotation is verbatim, following the citation from 118:22. The implicit point: Jesus the rejected son is the stone God is making the cornerstone.
12: ekratēsai auton / ephobēthēsan ton ochlon / parabolēn eipen pros autous — to seize him / feared the crowd / spoken the parable against them — ekratēsai (to seize, arrest) — the desire is frustrated by crowd dynamics. The leaders' recognition that pros autous (against them) the parable was aimed shows their interpretive accuracy, which is ironic: they understand the parable better than they would like.
13: Pharisaioi kai Hērōdianoi / agreusosin — Pharisees and Herodians / to trap/snare him — the alliance of Pharisees and Herodians is unusual — they represent opposing political orientations — but they were allied earlier in a plot against Jesus (3:6). Agreusosin is a hunting term (agreuo = to catch prey), revealing the predatory intent beneath the flattering opening words.
14: ou melet soi peri oudenos / hodos tou theou / kēnsos — it does not concern you about anyone / the way of God / census-tax — ou melet soi (it is no concern to you = you do not regard persons) is ironic flattery preparing the trap. Hodos tou theou (the way of God, or: God's way) — the phrase has an existential ring. Kēnsos is the Latin census borrowed into Greek — the annual Roman head-tax (*tributum capitis*) that was politically explosive for Jews, since paying it acknowledged Rome's sovereignty over the land of Israel.
15: hypokrisin / dēnarion — hypocrisy / denarius — hypokrisin (hypocrisy, play-acting) — Jesus sees through the performance immediately. The dēnarion (denarius) was a silver Roman coin worth a day's wage for a laborer. It bore the image (eikōn) and inscription (epigraphē) of the emperor. Jesus's request to see one places his questioners in a slight awkward position: they are already carrying the coin of the occupying power.
16: eikōn kai epigraphē — image and inscription — The denarius of Tiberius bore on the obverse his portrait with the inscription TI CAESAR DIVI AUG F AUGUSTUS (Tiberius Caesar, son of the divine Augustus, Augustus) and on the reverse PONTIF MAXIM (Pontifex Maximus). The coin thus made religious claims. Jesus's question forces the crowds to articulate whose image they have been using.
17: apodote ta Kaisaros Kaisari kai ta tou theou tō theō — give back to Caesar what is Caesar's and to God what is God's — apodote is render/give back (not merely give), suggesting the coin already belongs to Caesar and is being returned. The deeper irony: the eikōn tou theou (image of God) from Genesis 1:26–27 belongs to the one who bears it — human beings — so to give to God what is God's means to give oneself. The crowds are exethaumas-ed (greatly amazed).
18: Saddoukaioi / hoi legousin anastasin mē einai — Sadducees / who say there is no resurrection — Mark's parenthetical identification of the Sadducees (who accepted only the written Torah and rejected resurrection as a Pharisaic innovation) is written for readers who may not know them. The resurrection controversy is the theological center of this section.
19: leviratus / epigambreuō — levirate law — the levirate (from Latin levir, husband's brother) obligation is cited from Deuteronomy 25:5–6. The Greek verb epigambreuō (to marry the widow as kinsman-redeemer) appears in the LXX Deut 25:5. The law preserved the deceased brother's name and inheritance. The Sadducees' scenario is constructed to make the resurrection appear absurd.
20: hepta adelphoi — seven brothers — the number seven is rhetorically chosen for its completeness: the scenario exhausts every possibility before arriving at the widow. The Sadducees intend the scenario to produce a logical reductio ad absurdum of resurrection belief.
21: apethanen mē katalipōn sperma — died leaving no seed — sperma (seed, offspring) is the covenant word for descendants. Each brother dies childless (mē katalipōn sperma), which perpetuates the levirate obligation to the next brother. The repetition is deliberate rhetorical accumulation.
22: eschaton / kai hē gynē apethanen — finally / the woman also died — the legal chain is complete: all seven marriages ended without children, and at last the woman herself died. The phrase eschaton pantōn (last of all) echoes 12:6's description of the beloved son sent last. The scenario has been constructed with cold logic to press the resurrection question.
23: en tē anastasei... tinos autōn estai gynē — in the resurrection... whose wife will she be — the Sadducees' question assumes that resurrection life reproduces earthly institutional structures (marriage, kinship, legal obligation). Jesus's answer in v.25 dismantles this assumption entirely by describing the resurrection state in categorically different terms.
24: planasthe / mē eidotes tas graphas mēde tēn dynamin tou theou — you are in error / not knowing the Scriptures nor the power of God — Jesus diagnoses the Sadducees' error as double: (1) ignorance of Scripture — specifically the Torah they claim as their sole authority; (2) ignorance of God's power to create new modes of existence. The verb planasthe (you are led astray, you err) uses the same root as the false teachers/prophets who will deceive in 13:5–6, 22.
25: hōs angeloi en tois ouranois eisin — they are like the angels in heaven — the resurrection state is not a continuation of earthly relational-legal structures but something categorically different. Gamousin oute gamizontai (they neither marry nor are given in marriage) — the two Greek verbs distinguish active (marrying) and passive (being given in marriage, typically for women). The angel-comparison evokes immortality and pure worship-existence, not dis-embodiment.
26: epi tou batou / egō ho theos Abraam / Isaak / Iakōb — in the passage of the bush / I am the God of Abraham / Isaac / Jacob — Exodus 3:6 citation. Jesus argues from within the Torah — the Sadducees' own scripture. The key is the verb tense: God says egō eimi (I AM) the God of Abraham, not ēmēn (I was). Since God says this to Moses centuries after Abraham's death, Abraham must still be alive — actively in relationship with God. Jesus reads a resurrection-warrant out of the burning bush passage.
27: theos zōntōn, ou nekrōn / polu planasthe — God of the living, not of the dead / you greatly err — the aphorism crystallizes the argument: a God of the nekrōn (dead) would be god of the non-existent. The divine name is the pledge of resurrection. Polu planasthe (you greatly/widely err) is emphatic — the adverb intensifies the verdict.
28: grammateus / poia estin prōtē pantōn entolē — a scribe / which is the first of all commandments — this scribe functions differently from the hostile interlocutors of 12:13–27. He comes because he has heard Jesus debate well (kalōs apekrythē, he answered well) and asks an authentic question. The rabbinic debate about which commandment was greatest (prōtē / megalē) was genuine: attempts to identify the foundational principle of Torah were common in Second Temple Judaism.
30: agapēseis kyrion ton theon sou / kardia / psychē / dianoia / ischys — love the Lord your God / heart / soul / mind / strength — the Shema citation (Deut 6:4–5) in this verse lists four faculties; Mark's version adds dianoia (mind/understanding) to the Hebrew's three (heart, soul, strength). The LXX of Deut 6:5 varies: some versions give three terms, some four. The addition of dianoia (rational/cognitive faculty) may reflect a Hellenistic-Jewish amplification. Ex holēs (with all of) is repeated four times — the comprehensive commitment leaves no faculty exempt.
31: agapēseis ton plēsion sou hōs seauton — love your neighbor as yourself — Leviticus 19:18. The conjunction of Deut 6:4–5 and Lev 19:18 as the two supreme commandments is attested in contemporaneous Jewish sources (Testament of the Twelve Patriarchs; Philo). Meizon (greater, comparative) — Jesus describes these as greater than all other commandments; the superlative would be megistē. The comparison to all other commandments is implicit.
32: heis estin kai ouk estin allos plēn autou — he is one and there is no other besides him — the scribe's response affirms radical monotheism. He supplements Jesus's citation with an echo of Deut 4:35: ouk estin plēn autou. Notably, the scribe extends the application to ethics, linking love-commands to the prophetic critique of sacrifice (Hosea 6:6; 1 Sam 15:22).
33: holokautōmata kai thysias — whole burnt offerings and sacrifices — holokautōma (whole burnt offering — the entire animal burned) and thysia (sacrifice — peace offering where some is eaten). The scribe invokes the prophetic subordination of cultic acts to moral love. This is striking given the setting: the conversation is occurring in the temple precincts. The scribe implicitly aligns with the prophetic critique of pure ritualism.
34: nounechōs / ou makran ei apo tēs basileias tou theou — wisely/sensibly / you are not far from the kingdom of God — nounechōs (sensibly, with good sense) is a hapax legomenon in the NT (only here). Ou makran (not far) is a spatial metaphor — proximity without arrival. The scribe is not yet *in* the kingdom. The statement is simultaneously commendation and implicit invitation. After this exchange, no one dared (etolma) to ask any more questions.
35: Christos huios Daueid — the Messiah is the son of David — Jesus initiates this question himself in the temple courts. The scribal tradition identified the Messiah as a Davidic king (2 Sam 7:12–16; Isa 11:1). Jesus does not deny the Davidic connection but probes how it can be reconciled with Psalm 110:1, where David calls the Messiah kyrios.
36: eipen kyrios tō kyriō mou / kathō ek dexiōn mou — the Lord said to my Lord / sit at my right hand — Psalm 110:1 (LXX: eipen ho kyrios tō kyriō mou). This was the most-cited OT text in early Christianity. Jesus attributes the psalm to David, who spoke it en tō pneumati tō hagiō (by the Holy Spirit) — Mark's only use of this phrase for Davidic inspiration. The problem: if David calls the Messiah kyrios (Lord/Master), then the Messiah is David's superior, not merely his descendant.
37: autos Dauid legei auton kyrion / pothen autou estin huios — David himself calls him Lord / how is he his son — the rhetorical question stands without resolution in Mark. Jesus does not deny Davidic sonship but relativizes it: Davidic descent does not exhaust the Messiah's identity. The christological implication is that the Messiah is simultaneously Son of David (by lineage) and Lord of David (by divine nature). Ho polys ochlos ēkouen autou hēdeōs (the great crowd heard him gladly/with pleasure).
38: blepe / thelontōn en stolais peripatein — watch out / wanting to walk in long robes — blepe (watch, beware) opens the warning. Stolai (flowing robes, stoles) were distinctive garments marking high social status. Aspasmous en tais agorais (greetings in the marketplaces) — public salutation ceremonies affirmed social hierarchy.
39: prōtokathedrias / prōtoklisias — first seats / first reclining-places — prōtokathedria (first/chief seat in the synagogue, facing the congregation) and prōtoklisia (first place at a banquet couch, highest honor position) — both compound honor-markers. The scribes are depicted as performing religiosity for social advancement.
40: katesthiousin / chrēas tōn chērōn / perissoteron krima — they devour / houses of widows / more severe judgment — katesthiō (to devour, consume) is a vivid verb for economic exploitation. Widows were among the most economically vulnerable in first-century Jewish society (no inheritance rights, dependent on male family members). Prophasis (under pretext/for show) makes lengthy prayers. The word recalls the critique of performative piety. Perissoteron krima (more severe judgment/condemnation) — the gravity of their sin is proportional to their public prominence and exploitation of the vulnerable.
41: gazophylakion / theōrei / ochlos ballen chalkon — treasury / was watching / the crowd was throwing copper coins — gazophylakion (treasury, literally: the place where gaza [Persian: treasure] is kept). Jesus kathisas (having sat down opposite) — a teaching posture of deliberate attention. The imperfect eballen (they were throwing/kept throwing) is vivid and iterative, painting the ongoing stream of worshippers.
42: ptōchē chēra / lepta dyo ho estin kodrantēs — a poor widow / two lepta which is a quadrans — ptōchē (destitute/beggar-poor, not merely penēs, working-poor) combined with chēra (widow) marks the lowest economic status. Lepta (singular: lepton) were the smallest copper coins in circulation, worth 1/128 of a denarius. Mark explains them for Gentile readers as equaling a kodrantēs — the Latin quadrans, 1/4 of an as (the Roman copper unit). This translation note shows Mark writing for a Roman audience.
43: pleion pantōn beben tōn ballontōn eis to gazophylakion — she has given more than all those throwing into the treasury — Jesus's evaluation inverts the visible calculus. Pleion pantōn (more than all) — not in absolute amount but in relative cost. The verb beben (perfect of ballō) — she has definitively thrown in. The calling of the disciples (proskalesamenos) for this teachable moment signals the importance of what they are about to hear.
44: hyperisseuontos / hysterēseōs / holon ton bion autēs — from their abundance / from her poverty/lack / her whole livelihood — hyperisseuon (abundance, more than enough — from hyper + perisseuo) contrasts with hysterēsis (lack, want, deficiency). Bios (life, livelihood) — she gave not merely money but the material basis of her existence. The episode functions as a narrative counterpoint to the scribes who devour widows' houses (v.40): the widow, victim of such exploitation, gives everything, while her exploiters parade their piety.
